The quayside scene, with its warehouses, shipping agents' offices, and the masts of vessels in the inner basin, is treated with the ***same plein-air directness that **Monet** brought to pastoral subjects*** — the commercial port rendered as an atmospheric event rather than a documentary record. This 1872 date places the canvas in the same year as *Impression, Sunrise*, also painted at *Le Havre*. The *Musée Malraux* in *Le Havre* holds important canvases from **Monet**'s hometown campaigns.

**Monet** spent the winter and early spring of 1870-71 in *London* as a refugee from the Franco-Prussian War, and among the subjects he painted were the central *London* parks — *Green Park*, *St. James's Park*, and *Hyde Park* — that offered versions of the garden-and-figure subjects he had been developing in *Normandy* and *Argenteuil*. This view of *Hyde Park* captures the famous rolling grassland and mature elm trees in the particular quality of ***moist, silvery English winter park light*** that he found so different from anything in *France*. The *London* park paintings are important early evidence of **Monet**'s distinctive response to English atmospheric conditions — a response he would develop much further in the *Thames* series of 1899-1904. The *Rhode Island School of Design Museum* in *Providence* holds this canvas.

**Monet** painted *Impression, Sunrise* during a morning visit to the harbor of *Le Havre*, his hometown, around November 1872, depicting the port at dawn as fishing boats return through the orange light reflected on the still harbor water. When the painting was shown at the First Impressionist Exhibition in April 1874 at *Nadar*'s studio in *Paris*, the hostile critic *Louis Leroy* seized on its title to mock the entire show as the work of mere 'Impressionists' — and the movement had a name. **Monet** himself explained: 'They wanted a title for the catalogue, it couldn't really be taken for a view of Le Havre, and I said: *Put Impression.*'** A striking scientific curiosity confirms its impressionistic truth: the orange sun has the same luminance as the surrounding sky, disappearing completely in a grayscale conversion as if ***the sun itself exists only as a color sensation, not as a light source***. Now in the *Musée Marmottan Monet* — stolen in 1985 and recovered in 1990.

This very late iris painting, dated 1924-25, belongs to the final two years of **Monet**'s active picture-making before his death in December 1926. By this date his cataract surgery of 1923 had corrected his near-blindness but introduced new optical complications from his Zeiss corrective lenses, and his paintings show a slightly different color temperature from the pre-surgery work. ***The irises are reduced to pure strokes of blue, purple, and violet*** with a physical freedom that removes all descriptive function from the brushstroke and asks it to perform as pure color event. These final *Giverny* flower paintings are sometimes compared to **de Kooning**'s late work in their combination of extreme physical debilitation with undiminished creative energy. The *Musée Marmottan Monet* and private collections hold examples.

**Monet** painted this broad landscape view of the *Gennevilliers* agricultural plain on the north bank of the *Seine* loop — the area that would later figure in the *Argenteuil* period as the far bank of his river views — in 1867, before settling in the town. The wide, flat alluvial plain under a Normandy sky demonstrates, even at this early date, ***Monet's commitment to the most unpicturesque of landscape subjects: flat land under open sky, with no dramatic incident to justify the painting's existence except the quality of light***. The *Gennevilliers* plain view of 1867 is an important early example of the compositional audacity that would become fully characteristic in the Water Lily pond works.

**Monet** worked on the *Grandes Décorations* — the monumental series of Water Lily panels donated to the *French Republic* — from inception in 1915 through to his death in December 1926. *The Clouds* panel belongs to the first oval room at the *Musée de l'Orangerie*, where it forms part of an enveloping wall-to-wall sequence of water, flowers, and sky reflections. In this section the water surface is dominated by the soft reflections of cumulus clouds rather than by lily pads, the ***entire panel given over to the meeting of sky and water in a surface that is ceiling and floor simultaneously***. The *Orangerie* was inaugurated on May 17, 1927 — nearly five months after **Monet**'s death — and has since been *France*'s most visited art installation after the *Mona Lisa*. **Clemenceau** was present at the opening and wept.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1867_Jean Monet in His Cradle.jpg
artwork_description: **Monet** painted this tender portrait of his infant son *Jean* — born August 1867 — in the domestic space of the family's *Paris* apartment, capturing the sleeping child in the same spirit of direct, unsentimental observation that distinguished all his best work. The canvas is exceptional in his oeuvre both for its intimate subject and for its quality of hovering parental attention — ***the painter's eye recording precisely what the father's love perceives***. *Jean* appears again as a small child in the *Argenteuil* garden paintings of the early 1870s. The 1867 date places this among the most important of **Monet**'s early figure subjects. The *Ny Carlsberg Glyptotek* in *Copenhagen* holds this important early portrait.

**Monet** worked in the *Fontainebleau forest* in 1864-65 alongside the *Barbizon* painters who had made the forest their canonical subject for thirty years. This forest road view, with its receding avenue of oaks disappearing into the midground, engages directly with the *Barbizon* tradition of ***the forest interior as a cathedral of natural light*** — shafts of sun through dense canopy, the road as nave, the oaks as columns. The format explicitly invokes *Rousseau*'s forest interiors and *Diaz de la Peña*'s forest light studies, while **Monet**'s more rapid, outdoor touch already points beyond the studio reworking characteristic of the *Barbizon* tradition. The *Ateneum* in *Helsinki* holds a related forest road from this period.

This 1867 seascape off *Sainte-Adresse*, made the same summer as the celebrated *Garden at Sainte-Adresse*, shows the *Channel* sea from the *Le Havre* foreshore with the characteristic steep, short chop of the *Norman* coast in fresh breezes. ***The choppy, grey-green sea is the opposite of Mediterranean decorativeness*** — it is a working sea, functional and dangerous, whose light and motion are observed with careful meteorological accuracy. The 1867 *Sainte-Adresse* seascapes belong to the most ambitious group of canvases in **Monet**'s early career — works in which he was testing himself simultaneously against history painting, plein-air naturalism, and the marine tradition of the *Dutch* masters. The *Clark Art Institute* in *Williamstown* holds a related *Sainte-Adresse* marine view.

This canvas from the *Gare Saint-Lazare* series, now in the *Lower Saxony State Museum* in *Hannover* (W448), focuses on one of the railway signal gantries within the station — the mechanical apparatus of train management — rather than on the departing trains that dominate the more celebrated versions. The signal posts create ***a delicate structural counterpoint to the steam clouds that fill the iron-and-glass canopy above***, and the empty platform beside them implies the imminent arrival or departure of the train that requires their direction. The 1877 *Saint-Lazare* series represents **Monet**'s single most concentrated campaign of industrial subject matter — the twelve canvases made in four months constitute a complete survey of the station's visual possibilities.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1877_Exterior of Saint-Lazare Station, Sunlight Effect.jpg
artwork_title: Exterior of Saint-Lazare Station, Sunlight Effect
artwork_description: Unlike the interior steam-hall views that dominate the *Gare Saint-Lazare* series, this canvas looks outward from beside the station to the exterior — the back streets, neighboring buildings, and the open sky above the station environs — in sunlight rather than the enclosed steam atmosphere of the canopy. The exterior view is exceptional within the series for its relatively conventional subject: ***a city street in afternoon sunlight rather than the unprecedented industrial intimacy of steam and iron that made the interior canvases so radical***. **Monet** worked on these exterior studies from the pavement outside the station. Now in a private collection (W443), this exterior view is one of the rarest and least exhibited of the twelve *Saint-Lazare* canvases.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1884_Portrait of the Artist in His Studio.jpg
artwork_title: Portrait of the Artist in His Studio
artwork_description: This self-portrait shows **Monet** in his *Giverny* studio surrounded by canvases — one of only a handful of self-portraits in his entire career, making it an exceptional document. He stands before his easel as ***both the maker of art and the subject of art***, the studio environment rendering visible the conditions of production that his finished plein-air canvases typically suppress. The portrait was probably made during the winter studio season between outdoor campaigns, when **Monet** worked up and finished paintings begun in the field. At forty-three, the year after moving to *Giverny*, he appears confident and purposeful — a man about to enter his greatest decade. Private collection; rarely exhibited publicly.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1872_Small Boat on the Small Branch of the Seine at Argenteuil.jpg
artwork_title: Small Boat on the Small Branch of the Seine at Argenteuil
artwork_description: **Monet** explored the backwaters, narrow channels, and secondary branches of the *Seine* at *Argenteuil* as thoroughly as the main river, finding in the quieter water the reflective conditions he preferred for studying the interaction of boat, water, and sky. This small boat on the small arm — perhaps his own dinghy moored near the house he rented from 1871-78 — shows ***the intimate domestic scale of riverine life as opposed to the regatta splendor of the main channel***. The narrow arm and its still surface anticipate the *Epte* backwater and ultimately the water garden pond. The *National Museum of Western Art* in *Tokyo* holds a closely related *Argenteuil* small-arm canvas from this inaugural year.

This harbor view, dated around 1873, shows the mix of commercial and pleasure vessels that populated the *Seine* ports and river basins that **Monet** documented throughout the *Argenteuil* years. The clustering of masts and hulls in a harbor basin creates a subject of considerable compositional complexity — the rigging and spars of multiple vessels interlocking in a pattern that is ***both geometrically precise and atmospherically dissolved by the river-harbor light***. The harbor subjects from the early 1870s connect **Monet** to the Dutch marine tradition, but his treatment suppresses the anecdotal and concentrates entirely on the tonal and chromatic event. These harbor views from around 1873 were appreciated by collectors through *Durand-Ruel*.

This winter view of *Argenteuil* under snow from the winter of 1874-75 shows **Monet** returning to the snow subject he had first explored in the early 1870s with renewed confidence and chromatic ambition. The late-1874 snowfall after the first Impressionist exhibition finds him in his most technically assured phase — the blue shadow on snow, the warm white of the freshly fallen surface, the grey-brown of exposed branch and wall are handled with ***a control that makes the canvas look effortless while concealing its considerable demanded skill***. These *Argenteuil* snow views of 1874-75 were among the first of his winter paintings to attract serious collector attention.

This 1890 view of the *Giverny* countryside in spring, painted in the year **Monet** purchased the *Giverny* house and began the serious redesign of the garden, captures the particular tender quality of the *Normandy* spring — pale greens, pastel blossom, and the ***silvery, moisture-laden light of April in the upper Seine valley***. The 1890 date also marks the threshold of the major series campaigns — the *Haystacks* series began that same autumn — and these spring views belong to the last moment before **Monet**'s working practice became decisively organized around the serial concept. The *Springtime in Giverny* canvases were sold through *Durand-Ruel* and are now distributed among private *European* and *American* collections.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1880_Sunset on the Seine at Lavacourt, Winter Effect.jpg
artwork_title: Sunset on the Seine at Lavacourt, Winter Effect
artwork_description: **Monet** submitted this sunset view of the *Seine* at *Lavacourt* in its winter ice-and-snow condition to the *1880 Salon*, where it was accepted and exhibited — his last Salon appearance after a decade away from the official exhibition. The painting shows the river in the warm gold-pink light of a winter sunset, the ice-flecked water carrying the chromatic drama of the sky in a series of broken reflections. ***The combination of winter bleakness and sunset warmth creates the emotional paradox that **Monet** found most characteristic of the Vétheuil landscape***: beauty in desolation, warmth in cold. This was the canvas selected for official sanction at a time when the critics were still uncertain about Impressionism's legitimacy. The *Musée du Petit Palais* in *Paris* holds this important Salon picture.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1878_The Arm of the Seine.jpg
artwork_description: **Monet** painted the small subsidiary arms and channels of the *Seine* between *Argenteuil* and *Vétheuil* as generously as the main river, finding in the quieter, shaded water the most ChromaticColor complex conditions for reflective surface painting. This 1878 view of a Seine arm — painted in the transitional period between his departure from *Argenteuil* and his arrival at *Vétheuil* — shows the narrow channel crowded with summer vegetation on both banks, the still water between them capturing sky and leaf in a palette ***blue in the center, progressively darkened by the overhanging green on either side***. The Seine arm subjects of 1878 presage the willow-and-water views of the *Giverny* water garden by fifteen years.

The yacht basin at *Argenteuil* — where the *Cercle de la Voile de Paris* moored its racing fleet — was among **Monet**'s most frequently painted subjects during the *Argenteuil* years, the white sails and blue water of the yachting world providing an endlessly variable chromatic subject that combined the natural and the modern in exactly the spirit of early Impressionism. This 1874 view, made in the year of the first Impressionist exhibition, shows the basin with several sailing vessels at rest — the white hulls and reflected masts creating ***the characteristic Argenteuil vocabulary of geometry and luminosity*** that defines the period. The *Musée d'Orsay* holds several significant *Argenteuil* basin canvases.

**Monet** made this watercolor study of the *Tuileries* gardens as preparation for the related oil paintings made during the same 1876 campaign, using the lighter medium's transparency to capture the particular way park light filters through the chestnut canopy in spring or early summer. Watercolor enabled him to capture the first impression more rapidly than oil, establishing the tonal structure and key color relationships that he would then resolve more fully in the finished painting. ***The watercolor retains a freshness of abbreviation that the finished oil canvas necessarily develops further but can never fully recapture***. These rare watercolor studies from the 1876 *Paris* campaign are exceptionally important documents of **Monet**'s working method.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1885_Winter Landscape at the Val de Falaise.jpg
artwork_title: Winter Landscape at the Val de Falaise
artwork_description: The *Val de Falaise* — a minor valley in the *Norman* hills inland from the coast — provides the subject for this restrained winter landscape of 1885, a painting that demonstrates **Monet**'s consistent interest in the specifically *Norman* winter landscape of bare fields, bare trees, and low grey sky that he had been painting since the early 1870s. The chalk hillsides under grey cloud create ***the palette of absolute restraint — grey-white, bone-grey, and the warm brown-grey of bare earth*** — that he associated with the interior of the *Norman* landscape as opposed to the dramatic seascapes of the coast. These quiet inland winter views from the mid-1880s are among the least exhibited of his work but demonstrate the full range of his sustained engagement with his home region.

artwork_file: Claude Monet_1865_Bazille and Camille (Study for Déjeuner sur l'Herbe).jpg
artwork_title: Bazille and Camille (Study for Déjeuner sur l'Herbe)
artwork_description: **Monet** made this watercolor study in preparation for his magnificent, ultimately abandoned large canvas of *Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe* — the modern *fête champêtre* project of 1865-66 that would have consolidated his claim to Salon seriousness. The watercolor shows **Frédéric Bazille** and Camille in the *Fontainebleau forest* setting, rehearsing the poses that would populate the monumental canvas. ***The spontaneity of the watercolor medium captures the vivid freshness of outdoor observation that the large oil — though never completed — would have attempted to formalize***. **Bazille** was killed in the Franco-Prussian War in 1870 at age twenty-eight, depriving Impressionism of one of its most gifted members. The *Kunsthalle Bremen* holds this rare and historically important watercolor study.
